Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex This scenario depicts a multi-stage cyber attack targeting critical water infrastructure, beginning with reconnaissance and escalating through various techniques to achieve persistent access and disruptive impacts.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Potential motives include espionage, sabotage, or financial gain, exploiting vulnerabilities in industrial control systems.

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Process, Social Media Platform, Internet-facing assets, Command, Windows Registry, Logon Session, OS Process, User Account, File, Network Traffic
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Reg.exe Utility, Spearphishing via Service, Delivery Method, Command Execution (Mechanism), Windows Registry Key Creation (Mechanism), Logon Session Creation (Mechanism), Process Hollowing, User Account Authentication (Mechanism), Windows Registry Key Access (Mechanism), File Modification (Mechanism), Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: Adversaries employ network sniffing using Reg.exe utility to intercept and analyze raw network packets, gathering information on open ports, services, and communication patterns within water/wastewater systems to identify potential entry points and vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: In this phase, attackers develop tailored spearphishing campaigns by crafting deceptive emails or messages linked to social media platforms, exploiting human psychology to trick employees into revealing credentials or clicking malicious links relevant to water treatment operations.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-555: Remote Services with Stolen Credentials
stage.description: By leveraging stolen credentials obtained earlier, adversaries compromise internet-facing remote desktop services, allowing unauthorized access to water facility networks where default configurations or unpatched vulnerabilities facilitate easy entry.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC-9: Buffer Overflow in Local Command-Line Utilities
stage.description: Once inside the system, attackers execute indirect commands through manipulated inputs or insecure interfaces, enabling them to run malicious scripts or code that disrupt monitoring and control functions in the water distribution infrastructure.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: To maintain long-term access, adversaries establish scheduled tasks by creating registry keys that trigger periodic execution of malware payloads, ensuring the attack remains active even after system reboots or routine maintenance in the water plant environment.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Attackers elevate their privileges by targeting local accounts with weak or default passwords, particularly on internal workstations, using footprinting techniques to discover high-privilege sessions and manipulate them for greater control over SCADA systems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC-671: Requirements for ASIC Functionality Maliciously Altered
stage.description: By employing process hollowing, adversaries hide malicious activities within legitimate processes, evading detection from basic monitoring tools by mimicking normal system operations and altering hardware-related behaviors subtly.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: After gaining access, attackers crack or retrieve user account credentials from accessible storage locations, stealing login details to infiltrate more secure parts of the water treatment network and expand their operational scope.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Utilizing network sniffing again with Reg.exe utility, adversaries discover key assets and system mappings by analyzing ongoing communications, helping them locate vulnerable components like control room PCs or SCADA controllers for further exploitation.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC-226: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: With stolen credentials now in hand, attackers move laterally across the network by manipulating Windows registry keys to impersonate valid user sessions, accessing shared files or remote desktop connections to spread the attack to other segments of the water infrastructure.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: During collection, adversaries modify files or use mechanisms to extract sensitive data such as process control parameters or operational schedules from infected systems, aiming to gather intelligence on daily water treatment routines without immediate destruction.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Captured data is sent out through scheduled transfers using manipulated network traffic flows, possibly via simple protocols like SMB or FTP, to exfiltrate information securely and undetected from the water facility's network to external command-and-control servers.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC-681: Exploitation of Improperly Controlled Hardware Security Identifiers
stage.description: Focused on causing harm, attackers alter operating modes by sending modified network traffic contents, disrupting automated controls in SCADA systems to adjust flow rates or pump operations, potentially leading to service outages or physical safety incidents in the water supply chain.

Scenario.summary: This complex attack campaign demonstrates how basic-skilled threat actors can systematically breach water and wastewater systems starting from reconnaissance, using social engineering and exploited services to gain and maintain access, ultimately leading to significant operational disruptions.
